steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has two clauses joined by the concessive conjunction "though." The left clause is complete: "Such interpretations are ultimately [speculative]." The right clause is also complete: "the object's gray coloration may be an incidental effect..." We need punctuation that correctly links these two clauses using "though" as a connector.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘speculative, though;’: The comma after "speculative" attaches "though" as a trailing connector to the first clause, and the semicolon then properly separates what follows as a new independent clause. This is the standard way to use "though" as a sentence connector: [clause], though; [clause]. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘speculative, though,’: Placing commas on both sides of "though" sets it off as a parenthetical, making it a mid-sentence aside rather than a clause connector. Without the semicolon, the two full clauses are joined only by a comma — a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘speculative; though’: A semicolon before "though" creates a hard break, but "though" then opens the second clause without the punctuation needed after it. The result is "though the object's gray coloration may be..." — a dependent clause floating after a semicolon, which requires an independent clause. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘speculative, though’: The comma after "speculative" is correct, but no semicolon follows "though," so the two independent clauses are connected only by the comma — another comma splice. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
